title: The Forgiving Majority
passage:
The trouble with right-handed tools, the ergonomist Nadia Bruck argues, is rarely the tool. Testing volunteers on can openers, ladles and school scissors, her team at the Vellum workshop found that left-handers fumbled the scissors badly — the upper blade hides the cut line from the left hand — but managed the opener and the ladle almost as smoothly as anyone once they were allowed to turn the object rather than the hand. The scissors, she notes, force a grip that cannot be reversed; the opener and the ladle only invite one. Designers who set out to cure left-handed frustration, Bruck suggests, tend to make mirrored copies of everything in sight, when the cheaper remedy is to pick out the few tools that genuinely lock the hand into a single approach and to leave the forgiving majority alone.
— Owen Rask

questions.prompt: What distinction does the text draw between the scissors and the other tools that were tested?

options.text: The opener and the ladle gave left-handers more difficulty than the scissors did, because those tools demand steadier force from the weaker hand.
All three tools frustrated the left-handed volunteers about equally, right up until they were finally allowed to hold the hand still and turn the object.
The scissors force one grip that cannot be reversed, whereas the opener and the ladle can be managed by turning the object rather than the hand.
The scissors were the only genuinely right-handed tool in the test, which is why the left-handers struggled so much more with them.

questions.rationale: Family ELF-TYPE-001 (direct detail retrieval), short text. STEM-LAW: the stem asks what distinction is drawn but does not entail which side of it the scissors fall on. WHY-KEY (C): the passage states that left-handers 'fumbled the scissors badly' but 'managed the opener and the ladle almost as smoothly as anyone once they were allowed to turn the object rather than the hand', and glosses it — 'The scissors ... force a grip that cannot be reversed; the opener and the ladle only invite one.' C paraphrases exactly that contrast. WHY-DISTRACTORS: A = direction reversal — flips which tools were hard (the text says the scissors were the problem, not the opener and ladle) and adds an unstated force explanation; tempting to a reader who recalls only that some tools were awkward. B = quantifier_upgrade — 'all three ... about equally' erases the very distinction the passage builds, which singles the scissors out; tempting because turning the object really did help. D = outside_knowledge / surface_word_match — invents that the scissors were 'the only genuinely right-handed tool' (all three are ordinary tools tested for handedness) and pins the struggle on that, whereas the text pins it on the unreversible grip and the hidden cut line; tempting because the scissors were indeed the hardest.

generator_meta.topic: left-handedness and everyday tool design: an ergonomist's finding that most 'right-handed' tools frustrate left-handers only because of learned approach, and that just a few tools (scissors) truly lock the hand, so mirrored copies of everything are the wrong remedy

fictional_source.byline: Owen Rask (invented journalist); research attributed to invented ergonomist Nadia Bruck

fictional_entities: Nadia Bruck, ergonomist and study lead (invented; all findings invented)
generator_meta.spelling_note: BrE held throughout ('workshop', 'cure', 'colour'-family neutral); no AmE-only spellings present.
generator_meta.engineered_traps: direction reversal at A (which tools were hard, plus an unstated force reason), quantifier_upgrade at B ('all three equally' erases the singled-out scissors), outside_knowledge/surface_word_match at D ('only genuinely right-handed tool'); key C is the balanced paraphrase and is NOT the sole hedged option — A and B are stated as confident, absolute claims and D as a single-cause claim, so 'pick the qualified answer' does not select the key.
generator_meta.length_tell_discipline: Options run 22-26 words: A 23, B 25, C 26 (key), D 22. Key C is the longest option, but only by a single word over the B distractor (25); the margin is negligible and option_length_ratio 26/22 = 1.18 is well under the 2.36 ELF cap, so length is not a usable tell.
generator_meta.self_blind_solve: Solved from the passage alone: C is the only option consistent with 'force a grip that cannot be reversed; the opener and the ladle only invite one' and with left-handers managing the opener/ladle once allowed to turn the object. A reverses which tools were hard; B denies the distinction the text draws; D invents a wrong cause. Exactly one defensible answer. All entities fictional; no famous-thesis anchoring.
